288	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
289	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
290	
291	\begin{definition}
292	
293	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
294	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
296	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
297	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
298	  \[
299	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
300	  \]
301	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
302	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
303	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
304	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
305	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
306	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
307	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
308	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
309	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
310	  canonical compatibility
311	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
312	     \;\cong\;
313	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
314	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
315	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
316	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
317	
318	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
319	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
320	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
321	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
322	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
323	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
324	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
325	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
326	\end{definition}
327	
328	\begin{lemma}
329	
330	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
331	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
333	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
334	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
335	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
336	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
337	  \[
338	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
339	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
340	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
341	  \]
342	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
343	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
344	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
345	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
346	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
347	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
348	  \[
349	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
350	    \qquad
351	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
352	  \]
353	  the associator
354	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
355	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
356	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
357	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
358	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
359	\end{lemma}
360	
361	\begin{proof}
362	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
363	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
364	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
365	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
366	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
367	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
368	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
369	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
370	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
371	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
372	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
373	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
374	\end{proof}
375	
376	\begin{remark}
377	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
378	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
380	        lem:whisker_of_W}
381	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
382	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
383	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
384	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
385	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
386	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
387	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
388	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
389	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
390	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
391	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
392	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
393	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
394	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
395	
396	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
397	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
398	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
399	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
400	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
401	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
402	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
403	  fixed-base presheaves in
404	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
405	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
406	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
407	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
408	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
409	  it.
410	
411	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
412	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
413	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
414	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
415	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
416	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
417	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
418	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
419	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
420	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
421	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
422	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
423	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
424	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
425	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
426	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
427	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
428	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
429	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
430	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
431	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
432	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
433	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
434	\end{remark}
435	
436	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
437	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
438	
439	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
440	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
441	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
442	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
443	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
444	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
445	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
446	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
447	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
448	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
449	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
450	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
451	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
452	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
453	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
454	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
455	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
456	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
457	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
458	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
459	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
460	to the off-path route~(e).
461	
462	\begin{lemma}
463	
464	\leanok
465	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
466	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
467	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
468	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
469	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
470	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
471	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
472	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
473	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
474	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
475	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
476	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
477	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
478	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
479	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
480	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
481	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
482	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
483	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
484	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
485	  \[
486	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
487	      \;\;:=\;\;
488	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
489	  \]
490	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
491	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
492	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
493	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
494	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
495	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
496	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
497	\end{lemma}
498	
499	\begin{proof}
500	
501	
502	\leanok
503	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
504	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
505	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
506	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
507	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
508	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
509	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
510	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
511	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
512	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
513	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
514	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
515	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
516	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
517	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
518	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
519	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
520	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
521	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
522	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
523	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
524	
525	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
526	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
527	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
528	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
529	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
530	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
531	\end{proof}
532	
533	\begin{lemma}
534	
535	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
536	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
537	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
538	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
539	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
540	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
541	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
542	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
543	  morphism
544	  \[
545	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
546	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
547	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
548	  \]
549	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
550	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
551	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
552	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
553	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
554	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
555	\end{lemma}
556	
557	\begin{proof}
558	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
559	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
560	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
561	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
562	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
563	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
564	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
565	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
566	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
567	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
568	  isomorphism}
569	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
570	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
571	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
572	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
573	  therefore extension of scalars along
574	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
575	
576	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
577	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
578	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
579	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
580	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
581	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
582	  the abstract pullback functor.
583	
584	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
585	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
586	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
587	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
588	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
589	   \;\cong\;
590	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
591	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
592	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
593	  comparison
594	  \[
595	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
596	      \;\longrightarrow\;
597	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
598	      \;\otimes\;
599	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
600	  \]
601	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
602	
603	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
604	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
605	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
606	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
607	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
608	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
609	  % "\begin{lemma}
610	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
611	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
612	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
613	  % \begin{enumerate}
614	  % \item The functor
615	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
616	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
617	  % \item The functor
618	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
619	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
620	  %  [\dots]
621	  % \end{enumerate}
622	  % \end{lemma}
623	  %  [\dots]
624	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
625	  % of functors, see
626	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
627	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
628	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
629	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
630	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
631	
632	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
633	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
634	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
635	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
636	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
637	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
638	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
639	
640	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
641	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
642	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
643	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
644	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
645	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
646	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
647	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
648	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
649	  \otimes_S N)\).
650	
651	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
652	  \[
653	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
654	  \]
655	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
656	  presheaf-level adjunction
657	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
658	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
659	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
660	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
661	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
662	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
663	  composing the resulting comparison with
664	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
665	
666	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
667	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
668	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
669	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
670	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
671	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
672	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
673	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
674	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
675	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
676	
677	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
678	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
679	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
680	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
681	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
682	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
683	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
684	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
685	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
686	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
687	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
688	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
689	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
690	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
691	\end{proof}
692	
693	\begin{lemma}
694	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
695	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
696	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
697	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
698	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
699	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
700	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
701	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
702	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
703	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
704	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
705	
706	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
707	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
708	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
709	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
710	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
711	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
712	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
713	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
714	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
715	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
716	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
717	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
718	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
719	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
720	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
721	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
722	
723	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
724	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
725	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
726	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
727	  restriction-of-scalars functor
728	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
729	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
730	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
731	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
732	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
733	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
734	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
735	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
736	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
737	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
738	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
739	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
740	
741	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
742	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
743	\end{lemma}
744	
745	\begin{lemma}
746	
747	\leanok
748	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
749	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
750	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
751	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
752	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
753	  product: the canonical map
754	  \[
755	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
756	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
757	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
758	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
759	  \]
760	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
761	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
762	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
763	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
764	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
765	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
766	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
767	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
768	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
769	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
770	\end{lemma}
771	
772	\begin{lemma}
773	
774	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
775	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
776	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
777	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
778	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
779	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
780	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
781	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
782	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
783	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
784	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
785	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
786	  Mathlib). Then:
787	  \begin{enumerate}
788	    \item the lax tensorator
789	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
790	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
791	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
792	    \item the lax unit
793	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
794	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
795	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
796	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
797	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
798	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
799	  \end{enumerate}
800	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
801	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
802	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
803	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
804	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
805	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
806	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
807	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
808	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
809	\end{lemma}
810	
811	\begin{proof}
812	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
813	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
814	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
815	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
816	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
817	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
818	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
819	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
820	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
821	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
822	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
823	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
824	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
825	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
826	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
827	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
828	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
829	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
830	
831	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
832	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
833	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
834	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
835	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
836	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
837	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
838	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise
839	  invertibility to the presheaf level; once that lift is in place,
840	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) is monoidal. This presheaf H2 lift is bounded and
841	  has no Mathlib-absent ingredient; the sole genuinely Mathlib-absent piece of
842	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is the presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
843	  (``H1'').
844	\end{proof}
845	
846	\begin{lemma}
847	
848	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
849	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
851	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
852	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
853	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
854	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
855	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
856	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
857	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
858	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
859	\end{lemma}
860	
861	\begin{proof}
862	
863	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
864	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
865	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
866	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
867	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
868	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
869	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
870	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
871	\end{proof}

883	\begin{lemma}
884	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.

886	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
887	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
888	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
889	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
890	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
891	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
892	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
893	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
894	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
895	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
896	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
897	  left-whiskered morphism
898	  \[
899	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
900	  \]
901	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
902	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
903	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
904	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
905	
906	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
907	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
908	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
909	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
910	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
911	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
912	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
913	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
914	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
915	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
916	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
917	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
918	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
919	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
920	\end{lemma}
921	
922	\begin{proof}
923	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
924	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
925	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
926	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
927	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
928	
929	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
930	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
931	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
932	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
933	  \[
934	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
935	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
936	  \]
937	
938	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
939	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
940	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
941	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
942	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
943	
944	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
945	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
946	  linear maps (Mathlib
947	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
948	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
949	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
950	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
951	
952	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
953	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
954	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
955	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
956	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
957	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
958	\end{proof}
959

962	approach to the associator. The group law on iso-classes consumes only the

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39	
40	\begin{definition}[Higher direct image]
41	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
44	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
45	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
46	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
47	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
48	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
49	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
50	  % the sheaf
51	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
52	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
53	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
54	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
55	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
56	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
57	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
58	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
59	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
60	  sheafification of the presheaf
61	  \[
62	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
63	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
64	  \]
65	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
66	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{Quasi-coherence of the higher direct images}

139	% this implies that each of the sheaves
140	% $R^pg_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent since it sits in the middle of a short
141	% exact sequence with a cokernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves
142	% on the left and a kernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves on the right.
143	% Whence $P(U \cup V)$ and the proof of (1) is complete."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
146	  Quasi-coherence is local on \(S\), and forming higher direct images commutes
147	  with restriction to open subschemes, so we may assume \(S\) is affine; then
148	  \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. We argue by the induction principle
149	  for quasi-compact opens of a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme: for a
150	  quasi-compact open \(U \subset X\) with \(a = f|_U\), let \(P(U)\) be the
151	  property that \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\) for every
152	  quasi-coherent module \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(U\) and every \(p \geq 0\); the
153	  desired statement is \(P(X)\).
154	
155	  \emph{Affine base.} If \(U\) is affine then \(a\) is an affine morphism, so
156	  \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) for \(p \geq 1\) by the relative affine vanishing of
157	  Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}, while
158	  \(R^0 a_*\mathcal{G} = a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent because the pushforward
159	  of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism is
160	  quasi-coherent. Hence \(P(U)\) holds.
161	
162	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(U \subset X\) be quasi-compact open, \(V \subset X\)
163	  affine open, and suppose \(P(U)\), \(P(V)\), \(P(U \cap V)\) hold. Writing
164	  \(a = f|_U\), \(b = f|_V\), \(c = f|_{U \cap V}\), \(h = f|_{U \cup V}\), the
165	  relative Mayer--Vietoris sequence
166	  \[
167	    0 \to h_*\mathcal{G} \to a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)
168	      \to c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V}) \to R^1 h_*\mathcal{G} \to \cdots
169	  \]
170	  exhibits each \(R^p h_*\mathcal{G}\) as an extension built from quasi-coherent
171	  sheaves \(R^p a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U)\), \(R^p b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)\),
172	  \(R^p c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V})\): it sits between the cokernel of a map of
173	  quasi-coherent sheaves and the kernel of a map of quasi-coherent sheaves, hence
174	  is quasi-coherent. Thus \(P(U \cup V)\) holds. By the induction principle
175	  \(P(X)\) holds, proving the lemma.
176	\end{proof}

178	\section{Vanishing of higher direct images along an affine morphism}
179	
180	\begin{lemma}[Relative affine vanishing]
181	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
182	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_affine_eq_zero}
183	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
184	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KG (Lemma
185	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing')
186	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L180--185).
187	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
188	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
189	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
190	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KG (``Relative affine vanishing'').}
191	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
192	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. If \(f\) is affine, then
193	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\).
194	\end{lemma}
195	
196	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
197	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
198	% the sheaf
199	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
200	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
201	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
202	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
203	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
204	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
205	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
206	% opens we win."
207	\begin{proof}
208	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
209	  By Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}, \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the
210	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),
211	  \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\). Since \(f\) is affine, the preimage \(f^{-1}(V)\) of
212	  any affine open \(V \subset S\) is affine. The higher cohomology of a
213	  quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine scheme vanishes, so
214	  \(H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\) and all
215	  affine \(V\). As the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), the sheafification of a
216	  presheaf that vanishes on a basis is zero; hence \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for
217	  all \(i \geq 1\).
218	\end{proof}

220	\section{Flat base change for the higher direct images}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}[Flat base change, $i \geq 1$ case]
223	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_higher}
224	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_higherDirectImage_isIso}
225	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
226	    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
227	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change"), the
228	  % $i \geq 1$ case (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
229	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
230	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
231	  % $$
232	  % \xymatrix{
233	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
234	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
235	  % }
236	  % $$
237	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
238	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
239	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
240	  % For any $i \geq 0$
241	  % \begin{enumerate}
242	  % \item the base change map of
243	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
244	  % is an isomorphism
245	  % $$
246	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
247	  % $$
248	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
249	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
250	  % \end{enumerate}"
251	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
252	  \(i \geq 1\) case.}
253	  Consider the cartesian square
254	  \[
255	    \begin{array}{ccc}
256	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
257	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
258	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
259	    \end{array}
260	  \]
261	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
262	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is
263	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then for every \(i \geq 1\) the canonical
264	  base-change map (the higher-degree analogue of the pushforward base-change map
265	  of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is an isomorphism
266	  \[
267	    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
268	    R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
269	  \]
270	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
271	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
272	  \(B\)-modules
273	  \[
274	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
275	    H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
276	  \]
277	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
278	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
279	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
280	\end{theorem}
281	
282	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
283	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
284	% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
285	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
286	% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
287	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
288	% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
289	% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
290	% (equality by
291	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
292	% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
293	% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
294	% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
295	% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
296	% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
297	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
298	%
299	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
300	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
301	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
302	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
303	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
304	% We are trying to show that the map
305	% $$
306	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
307	% $$
308	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
309	% is an isomorphism.
310	%
311	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
312	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
313	% $$
314	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
315	% $$
316	% see
317	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
318	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
319	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
320	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
321	% $$
322	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
323	% $$"
324	\begin{proof}
325	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
326	    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
327	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The assertion is local on \(S'\), so we
328	  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
329	  \(A \to B\) flat. By quasi-coherence of the higher direct images
330	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}), \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is
331	  the quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module associated to the \(A\)-module
332	  \(H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\), and likewise
333	  \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to the \(B\)-module
334	  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
335	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map is identified with the
336	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
337	  \[
338	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
339	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
340	  \]
341	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
342	
343	  \medskip
344	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
345	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
346	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness every finite
347	  intersection \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) is again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
348	  computes sheaf cohomology:
349	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\).
350	  Base changing the cover yields an affine open cover
351	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
352	  \(X_B\), with the same property
353	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
354	  affine case identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the
355	  original term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of
356	  cochain complexes
357	  \[
358	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
359	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
360	  \]
361	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, the functor \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and hence
362	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^i\) in any
363	  degree \(i \geq 1\) gives the desired isomorphism
364	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
365	
366	  \medskip
367	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
368	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
369	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated rather
370	  than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so flat base
371	  change holds for each of them. Comparing the two {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
372	  sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
373	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
374	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
375	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively, the
376	  separated case provides the term-by-term identification
377	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
378	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
379	  \(E_2\)-pages; flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through the
380	  spectral sequences to the abutment, giving
381	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) in every degree
382	  \(i \geq 1\). Translating back to sheaves on \(S'\) yields the asserted
383	  isomorphism \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\).
384	\end{proof}

1927	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1928	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1929	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1930	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1931	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1932	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1933	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1934	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1935	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1936	
1937	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1938	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1939	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1940	  \[
1941	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1942	    \quad
1943	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1944	    \quad
1945	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1946	    \quad
1947	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1948	  \]
1949	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1950	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1951	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1952	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1953	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1954	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1955	
1956	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1957	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1958	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1959	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1960	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1961	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1962	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1963	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1964	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1965	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1966	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1967	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1968	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1969	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1970	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1971	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1972	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1973	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1974	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1975	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1976	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1977	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1978	  pinned commit.
1979	
1980	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1981	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1982	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1983	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1984	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1985	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1986	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1987	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1988	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1989	
1990	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1991	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1992	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1993	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
1994	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
1995	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
1996	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
1997	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
1998	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
1999	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
2000	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
2001	  already supplies.
2002	\end{lemma}
2003	
2004	\begin{proof}
2005	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2006	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2007	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2008	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
2009	  Work directly on isomorphism classes of locally-trivial sheaves, as in Mathlib's
2010	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2011	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
2012	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
2013	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2014	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
2015	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
2016	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
2017	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
2018	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
2019	  read as an equality of iso-classes,
2020	  \[
2021	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
2022	    \quad
2023	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
2024	    \quad
2025	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
2026	    \quad
2027	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle\beta\rangle:
2028	  \]
2029	  associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors
2030	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2031	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
2032	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
2033	  This is the same one-isomorphism-per-law shape that Mathlib's
2034	  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
2035	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}) uses on a skeletal
2036	  monoidal category via its skeletality map \(\mathtt{hC}\), but we do \emph{not}
2037	  instantiate that construction: it requires a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\)
2038	  (and \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), which is
2039	  exactly the structure for the varying structure sheaf that is unavailable.
2040	  Finally every element is invertible: the dual \(L^{-1}\) of
2041	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong
2042	  \mathcal{O}_X\), so \([L^{-1}]\) is a two-sided inverse of \([L]\); equivalently,
2043	  \([L]\) is a unit precisely when \(L\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
2044	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried
2045	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). This assembles the abelian group ---
2046	  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}
2047	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2048	  exactly as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) on
2049	  iso-classes of invertible modules (there off a fixed-ring \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2050	  here by hand).
2051	
2052	  Alternatively (fallback), once \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} provides the full
2053	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) via
2054	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), the group is the units of the skeleton,
2055	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\), exactly the
2056	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
2057	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.
2058	\end{proof}

2060	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2061	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2062	
2063	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2064	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2065	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2066	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2067	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2068	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2069	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2070	
2071	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2072	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2073	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2074	\[
2075	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2076	  \;:\equiv\;
2077	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2078	\]
2079	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2080	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2081	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2082	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2083	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2084	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2085	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2086	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2087	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
2088	
2089	\begin{lemma}
2090	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible (flat) objects]
2091	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2092	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2093	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2094	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2095	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2096	  % modules), Lemma lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1 (an invertible module
2097	  % is locally free of rank one, when the stalks are local rings)
2098	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
2099	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2100	  % "\begin{lemma}
2101	  % \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
2102	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
2103	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
2104	  % If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
2105	  % the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
2106	  % \end{lemma}"
2107	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M,
2108	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
2109	  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in
2110	  particular any scheme) the converse holds, so on a scheme invertible
2111	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) locally free of rank one --- hence flat.}
2112	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2113	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2114	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2115	  \[
2116	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2117	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2118	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2119	  \]
2120	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2121	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2122	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2123	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2124	  coherence is ever required.
2125	\end{lemma}
2126	
2127	\begin{proof}
2128	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2129	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2130	  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
2131	  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
2132	  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
2133	  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
2134	  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
2135	
2136	  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
2137	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
2138	  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
2139	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
2140	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
2141	  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2142	  currently depends on it. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
2143	  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
2144	  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
2145	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
2146	  which is closed: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
2147	  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
2148	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
2149	  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
2150	  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
2151	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.
2152	
2153	  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
2154	  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
2155	  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
2156	  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
2157	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
2158	  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
2159	  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
2160	  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
2161	\end{proof}

2163	\begin{definition}
2164	[The invertibility-carrier Picard group \(\Pic X\)]
2165	  \label{def:pic_carrier}
2166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup}
2167	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
2168	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2169	  % modules), Definition definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
2170	  % iso-classes of invertible modules under tensor product)
2171	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4350-L4357).
2172	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2173	  % "\begin{definition}
2174	  % \label{definition-pic}
2175	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2176	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2177	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2178	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2179	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2180	  % \end{definition}"
2181	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
2182	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of
2183	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, with addition the tensor product.}
2184	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects
2185	  \[
2186	    \mathtt{Invertible}\,X
2187	    \;:=\;
2188	    \bigl\{\, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
2189	              \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
2190	  \]
2191	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and equip it with the setoid whose
2192	  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
2193	  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
2194	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
2195	  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
2196	  \[
2197	    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
2198	  \]
2199	  is the quotient type of isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2200	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
2201	  invertible \(M\). This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2202	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\), carried on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) rather than on
2203	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\); on a scheme the two predicates agree (Stacks tag
2204	  0B8M), but only the invertibility form carries the inverse witness existentially.
2205	\end{definition}

2233	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item 1): the tensor product of two
2234	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is again invertible.}
2235	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, M' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If

2240	\begin{proof}
2241	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible,
2242	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
2243	  Let \(N\) be a tensor inverse of \(M\) (so \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\))
2244	  and \(N'\) a tensor inverse of \(M'\) (so \(M' \otimes_X N' \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)),
2245	  furnished by the two invertibility witnesses. We claim \(N \otimes_X N'\) is a
2246	  tensor inverse of \(M \otimes_X M'\). Indeed, reassociating and commuting the four
2247	  factors with the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the
2248	  braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2249	  \[
2250	    (M \otimes_X M') \otimes_X (N \otimes_X N')
2251	    \;\cong\;
2252	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X (M' \otimes_X N')
2253	    \;\cong\;
2254	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X
2255	    \;\cong\;
2256	    \mathcal{O}_X,
2257	  \]
2258	  the middle step substituting the two witness isomorphisms and the last being the
2259	  unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}. Hence \(N \otimes_X N'\) witnesses
2260	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes_X M')\). Every step is consumed only as the
2261	  existence of an isomorphism, so no coherence identity is required.

2346	  with isomorphisms in place of equalities. Chain the unitor
2347	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the two hypotheses, the associator
2348	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the braiding
2349	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:

2406	  abelian group, with
2407	  \[
2408	    [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes_X M'],
2409	    \qquad
2410	    1 := [\mathcal{O}_X],
2411	    \qquad

2416	\begin{proof}
2417	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
2418	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2419	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2420	  Each group-operation datum and each axiom is supplied by a single
2421	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).
2422	
2423	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
2424	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
2425	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
2426	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2427	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
2428	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
2429	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
2430	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
2431	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
2432	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
2433	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
2434	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
2435	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
2436	
2437	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
2438	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
2439	  \[
2440	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
2441	    \quad
2442	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
2443	    \quad
2444	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
2445	    \quad
2446	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
2447	    \quad
2448	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
2449	  \]
2450	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
2451	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
2452	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
2453	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
2454	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
2455	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
2456	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
2457	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
2458	  for a two-sided inverse.
2459	
2460	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
2461	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
2462	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
2463	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
2464	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
2465	  thereby off this group's critical path.
2466	\end{proof}
